A $12.5B credit book and nearly $19B in AUM doesn't happen by accident. They are a giant flywheel that is accelerating each business segment faster. 

Meli is building a massive digital bank on top of a dominant e-commerce giant. As more and more people in Latin America have access to credit, this will fuel more people to shop online, growing e-commerce in a region that has always had historically low penetration.

Margins will expand naturally once the competition is starved out.

I have heard many people worried that $MELI getting its banking license in Mexico and eventually Argentina will result in banking-like multiples. I couldn't really disagree more with this take but I will break down why. MercadoLibre is not becoming a traditional commercial bank.

MercadoLibre has unique unit economics. Traditional banks spend massive amounts of money on physical branches and marketing campaigns to acquire new customers. Mercado Pago acquires users for nearly zero marginal cost. The company funnels millions of merchants and consumers directly from its massive ecom marketplace. And banking is a very sticky business that adds to the stickiness of MELI's whole ecosystem.

Operating costs are also radically lower for MercadoLibre. Legacy banks in Latin America pay over $100 annually to maintain an active account. By using a cloud-first, mobile-native architecture, Mercado Pago spends just ~$1.50 per account in Mexico. This hyper-efficiency allows the company to profitably serve low-income demographics and micro-merchants that traditional banks ignore.

MercadoLibre holds a massive advantage in risk assessment. Instead of relying on lagging, unreliable credit bureaus, the company uses real-time ecom cash flows, payment behaviors, and logistics data to underwrite loans. This proprietary data engine fueled a ~90% YoY surge in its credit portfolio while still making 20%+ margins.

A formal MBI license completely changes the company's cost of capital. Currently, Mercado Pago relies on expensive wholesale funding and securitization to fund its loans. With an MBI license, MercadoLibre can hold retail checking and savings deposits, which is the cheapest source of capital in the financial system. When $NU gained similar deposit authorization in Mexico in early 2026, its funding costs dropped by ~25%. MercadoLibre is positioned to aggressively scale this exact margin expansion.

The ultimate goal of this banking transition is payroll portability. When users direct their salary deposits to Mercado Pago, the platform graduates into their primary financial operating system. The capital stays entirely inside the corporate ecosystem. Users buy daily necessities with their Mercado Pago card, pay utility bills in the app, and spend their remaining discretionary income on the MercadoLibre ecom platform.

Credit expansion directly fuels ecom sales. When Mercado Pago issues a working capital loan to a merchant, the merchant buys more inventory to list on the platform. This expanded inventory attracts greater consumer traffic and drives up GMV. MercadoLibre earns a standard ecom take-rate on the sale while simultaneously collecting interest on the underlying loan.

Mexico currently represents the most critical battleground for this strategy. Approximately half of the Mexican population lacks a basic bank account, and ~80%+ of businesses still use cash as their primary collection method. Mercado Pago officially initiated the application process for an MBI license in the third quarter of 2024 and expects a full-scale launch in 2026. By securing this top-tier license, MercadoLibre unlocks un-capped savings accounts, corporate loans, and direct payroll deposits.

$MELI and $NU are crushing the traditional banks in Brazil. But they are executing completely different strategies. 

Nubank is prioritizing absolute profitability. They hit a record 33% ROE And hit 131 million customers globally. Their current focus is extracting maximum yield from their mature user base as they grow in their less mature regions.

Mercado Pago is doing the exact opposite. They are weaponizing their balance sheet to buy market share. Their credit card portfolio grew 114% YoY. They aggressively flooded the market by issuing nearly 3 million new credit cards in just three months. MercadoLibre intentionally accepted lower margins to fund this massive expansion. The LTV of owning a dominant marketplace and a dominant banking ecosystem is incredibly high.
